coredata.dc:title: Corn-straw-derived, pyridine-nitrogen-rich NCQDs modified Cu0.05Zn2.95In2S6 promoted directional electrons transfer and boosted adsorption and activation of CO2 for efficient photocatalytic reduction of CO2 to CO
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Jinhang
Hou, Yanping
Sun, Jiangli
Wei, Jingwen
Zhang, Shiming
Liang, Jiaxiang
Yu, Zebin
Zhu, Hongxiang
Wang, Shuangfei
coredata.dc:description: In this study, biomass-derived carbon quantum dots (CQDs) doped with different nitrogen sources were loaded on Cu0.05Zn2.95In2S6 (CZIS) to achieve efficient and highly selective CO2 photocatalytic reduction. Results of various characterizations demonstrated that CZIS@CQDs-T with the highest pyridine nitrogen content (30.4%) exhibited the best light utilization, the most efficient carriers’ separation. In-situ XPS demonstrated that NCQDs were conducive to promoting electron transferred directly from the Zn3In2S6 to NCQDs under light illumination. The CZIS@CQDs-T achieved a CO yield of 70.691 μmol·g−1·h−1 with selectivity of 100%, and the yield was 4.77 times higher than that of the CZIS. CO2-TPD showed that the CZIS@CQDs-T displayed the strongest adsorption capacity for CO2, and a weak adsorption capacity for CO. In-situ FTIR analysis showed COOH* was the key intermediates during CO2 photoreduction process. DFT calculation revealed that the limb-joined pyridine nitrogen of the CZIS had denser charge density when adsorbing CO2, and the adsorption energy of CO2 and desorption energy of CO were significantly enhanced compared with the CZIS. Based on the above analysis, possible pathways and mechanisms of CO2 reduction were inferred. This study provides inspiration for designing efficient photocatalysts for highly selective reduction of CO2.

CZIS@CQDS-T
CO2 photoreduction
Charges transfer

UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (a); Light capture efficiency of different materials (LHE) (b); Tauc plots (c) of pure CZIS, CZIS@CQDs and CZIS@CQDs-T; Mott-Schottky plots (d); and Band structure diagram (e).

Photocatalytic CO2 reduction performance towards CO evolution from different samples (a, b); Effects of different influencing factors on photoreduction of CO2 (c); CO2 reduction cycling experiment (d).

head.ce:title: Corn-straw-derived, pyridine-nitrogen-rich NCQDs modified Cu0.05Zn2.95In2S6 promoted directional electrons transfer and boosted adsorption and activation of CO2 for efficient photocatalytic reduction of CO2 to CO

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, biomass-derived carbon quantum dots (CQDs) doped with different nitrogen sources were loaded on Cu0.05Zn2.95In2S6 (CZIS) to achieve efficient and highly selective CO2 photocatalytic reduction. Results of various characterizations demonstrated that CZIS@CQDs-T with the highest pyridine nitrogen content (30.4%) exhibited the best light utilization, the most efficient carriers’ separation. In-situ XPS demonstrated that NCQDs were conducive to promoting electron transferred directly from the Zn3In2S6 to NCQDs under light illumination. The CZIS@CQDs-T achieved a CO yield of 70.691 μmol·g−1·h−1 with selectivity of 100%, and the yield was 4.77 times higher than that of the CZIS. CO2-TPD showed that the CZIS@CQDs-T displayed the strongest adsorption capacity for CO2, and a weak adsorption capacity for CO. In-situ FTIR analysis showed COOH* was the key intermediates during CO2 photoreduction process. DFT calculation revealed that the limb-joined pyridine nitrogen of the CZIS had denser charge density when adsorbing CO2, and the adsorption energy of CO2 and desorption energy of CO were significantly enhanced compared with the CZIS. Based on the above analysis, possible pathways and mechanisms of CO2 reduction were inferred. This study provides inspiration for designing efficient photocatalysts for highly selective reduction of CO2.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Large amount of CO2 emission into the atmosphere has caused serious global warming and is posing serious threat to sustainable development of human society[1–4]. Therefore, reasonable solutions are urgently needed to reduce CO2 emissions and convert CO2 into valuable chemical products [5–7]. Generally, CO2 reduction usually requires high energy input since CO2 is a thermodynamically stable linear molecule with a C = O bond with high energy of 750 kJ mol−1 [8–10]. With driving force of solar energy, photocatalytic reduction of CO2 into value-added chemicals (CO, CH4, CH3OH, etc.) is a promising technology to alleviate the issue [11–15].

The ZnmIn2S3+m (m = 1–5) are reduction semiconductors with suitable band gaps and high reduction potentials, which have been widely used in photocatalytic reduction reactions [16–18]. However, the easy recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs of pristine ZnmIn2S3+m would lead to deterioration of photocatalytic activity [19,20]. In addition, photogenic holes would oxidize surface sulfide ions, leading to photo-corrosion, thus reducing photocatalytic activity and deteriorating stability [21]. Therefore, certain strategies are required to improve photocatalytic activity.

Metal doping into semiconductors could not only enhance photocatalytic activity, but also affect CO2 reduction products selectivity [22,23]. It has been demonstrated that copper (Cu)-based photocatalysts can promote the production of COOH*, resulting in high selectivity of CO or CH4 [24]. Xu et al. found that doping Cu in the layered dihydroxides of MgAl could adjust the band gap, promote electron transfer and obtain lower CO release energy, resulting in highly active CO2 photoreduction [25]. Wang et al. also found that doping Cu atoms in the ZnIn2S4 nanosheets could ensure higher charge density and more efficient carriers transport, thus achieving better photo-reducing activity [26]. However, excessive metal doping may lead to the damage of crystal structure of the semiconductor, negatively affecting photocatalytic activity. Therefore, it is necessary to optimize the proportion of metal doping to achieve desired performance.

Quantum dots (QDs) modification is another effective strategy to improve the photocatalytic activity of photocatalysts[27]. Various QDs modified photocatalysts or QDs-based photocatalysts have been developed, such as Ag QDs-Bi2S3/SnIn4S8 composites [28], B-doped graphene quantum dots (BGQDs) modified Zn3In2S6/AgBr [29], N-doped carbon (NC) loaded CdS QDs[30], etc., and those results showed that QDs could play the roles of promoting excitation of surface electrons and migration of interfacial electrons, or broadening photo-responsive range, promoting charges transfer, or serving as active sites to enhance adsorption and conversion of target reactants. Our group synthesized Cu doping and Ti3C2OH QDs co-modified Zn3In2S6, and achieved CO yield of 51.848 μmol·g−1·h−1 and CO selectivity of 99% owing to the synergistic action of Cu-doping and Ti3C2OH QDs modification [31]. However, preparation of Ti3C2OH QDs usually suffers from high operational risk, low product yield, and high material cost. Besides, Ti3C2OH QDs showed a weak improvement in optical response. Therefore, it is desired to develop highly efficient and cost-effective QDs to modify semiconductors.

Biomass-derived carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have shown great application potentials due to their great features of excellent electrical conductivity, unique optical properties, environmental friendliness, easy preparation and easy modification. CQDs could be used as photosensitizers to enhance light utilization ability [32]. Heteroatoms doping could further optimize the properties of CQDs. Due to the difference in electronegativity between nitrogen and carbon, introducing nitrogen into CQDs could result in charge asymmetry and redistribution of electron density, thereby optimizing the conductivity of CQDs [33,34]. Studies showed that due to the electron-rich nature of the N atom, N-doped CQDs could increase HOMO energy, further significantly reduce the LUMO-HOMO gap of CQDs, resulting in emission redshift, and thus improving optical properties of CQDs [35]; Besides, NCQDs could also promote charges transfer and separation[36]. Usually, for N-doped CQDs, different nitrogen sources would result in different proportions of pyrrole nitrogen, pyridine nitrogen and graphitic nitrogen in CQDs, and they could play different roles in photocatalytic activity. Further understandings on the effects of nitrogen states and contents on photocatalytic activity of CO2 reduction are still desired [37,38], which could be the key to design and optimize photocatalysts.

In this study, aiming at shedding light on the effects of N-doped CQDs modification and Cu-doping on photocatalytic activity of Zn3In2S6 toward CO2 reduction, the Cu0.05Zn2.95In2S6@CQDs-X (CZIS@CQDs-X) were prepared. The surface structural properties, photoelectric properties and photocatalytic activity of the CZIS@CQDs-X were characterized. The composite material with tryptophan as nitrogen source showed the highest pyridine nitrogen content. With the increase of pyridine nitrogen content, the performance of photocatalytic reduction of CO2 improved. The loading of CQDs could enhance light capture ability, and the weak alkalinity of pyridine nitrogen in the N-CQDs was favored of CO2 adsorption. N-doping leaded to charge asymmetry and electron density redistribution, which could accumulate electrons more effectively during the adsorption of CO2 and promote activation and subsequent product recombination reactions. The mechanism of CO2 reduction was discussed based on In-situ FT-IR analysis and DFT calculation.

All chemicals were used as purchased without further purification. The reagents and solvents included: Indium (III) chloride tetrahydrate (InCl3·4H2O), zinc sulfate heptahydrate (ZnSO4·7H2O), thioacetamide (CH3CSNH2, TAA), copper (II) chloride dihydrate (CuCl2·2H2O), maize straw (40 mesh), ethylenediamine (C2H8N2), urea (CH4N2O) and tryptophan (C11H12N2O2). They were all purchased from Aladdin Reagent Company, China. Deionized (DI) water was used for the synthesis and CO2 reduction experiment.
The synthesis of Zn3In2S6 was the same as our previous report [31].

Synthesis of CQDs: Firstly, 2.00 g of maize straw powder was poured into 40 mL of DI water, and stirred for 60 min. The solution was then poured into a stainless-steel autoclave lined with para polybenzene (PPL), and reacted at 200 °C for 4 h. Finally, the brown CQDs solution was obtained through collected, centrifuged and dialyzed of the supernatant.

Synthesis of NCQDs-X: 5.00 mmol of nitrogen source (ethylenediamine or urea or tryptophan) was added into 40 mL of maize straw suspension and stirred for 60 min. The subsequent steps were consistent with the preparation of CQDs. These N-doped carbon were labeled as NCQDs-E, NCQDs-U and NCQDs-T, respectively.

Simply, 5 mL of CQDS-X solution was added to the precursor solution of Cu0.05Zn2.95In2S6 (which was synthesized as described in our previous study), continuous stirring for 10 min and then subjected to hydrothermal reaction. The samples were labeled as Cu0.05Zn2.95In2S6@CQDs (CZIS@CQDs), Cu0.05Zn2.95In2S6@CQDs-E (CZIS@CQDs-U), and Cu0.05Zn2.95In2S6@CQDs-X (CZIS@CQDs-T), respectively.

Various characterizations and photoelectrochemical tests were performed to analyze the properties of the as-prepared materials, including XRD, XPS, TEM, SEM and photoelectric performance test (IT, EIS, TRPL, PL), etc. Details were provided in Supporting information.
The experimental process of photocatalytic CO2 reduction was consistent with our previous study [31]. The detailed operation methods and flow chart were shown in Supporting information and Fig. S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Crystal structure, composition, and morphology of the materials
ce:section.ce:para: The XRD patterns of various materials were shown in Fig. 1 . Diffraction peaks at 21.7°, 28.5°, 47.3°, 52.6° and 56.4° were corresponded to crystal planes (005), (102), (110), (019) and (022) of the Zn3In2S6 (PDF# 80–0835), respectively [39]. Comparing crystal structures of CZIS@CQDs and CZIS@CQDs-X, it would be found that the addition of CQDs solution unchanged the crystal structure, indicating that CQDs were loaded on CZIS surface rather than doping into the CZIS.

The elemental composition of CZIS@CQDS-T was analyzed by XPS. As shown in Fig. 2 (a), all elements of Zn, S, In, Cu, C and N appeared in the XPS survey scan. The Zn 2p orbit was split into 2p3/2 (1022 eV) and 2p1/2 (1045.05 eV) (Fig. 2 (b)), and the In 3d peak (Fig. 2 (c)) was fitted into two peaks at 445.25 eV (3d5/2) and 452.85 eV (3d3/2), while the S 2p peak (Fig. 2 (d)) was divided into one pair of 2p3/2 (161.95 eV) and 2p1/2 (163 eV) peaks, and Cu 2p orbit (Fig. 2 (e)) was corresponded to 932.23 and 952.23 eV, standing for Cu2+ [25]. As shown in Fig. 2 (f), the C 1 s peaks at 284.15, 284.9 and 285.8 eV were corresponded to C-C, C-N and C = N bonds, respectively [40]. The appearance of C-N and C = N demonstrated the presence of pyridine nitrogen in the CQDs. The N 1 s orbitals of the CZIS@CQDS-X were analyzed for three different nitrogen sources (Fig. 2 (g-i)). The CZIS@CQDS-T with tryptophan as nitrogen source containing the highest content of pyridine-N (17.56%) with binding energy of 397.7 eV, pyrrole-N (the content of 57.77%) with binding energy of 399.2 eV, and graphite-N (24.67%) with binding energy of 400.5 eV. The CZIS@CQDS-U with urea as nitrogen source contained pyridine-N (13.51%) with binding energy of 397.2 eV, pyrrole-N (76.11%) with binding energy of 398.5 eV and graphite-N (10.38%) with binding energy of 400.4 eV. In addition, the CZIS@CQDS-E synthesized from tryptophan contained pyridine-N (11.28%) with binding energy of 397.3 eV, pyrrole-N (56.74%) with binding energy of 398.9 eV and graphite-N (31.98%) with binding energy of 400.5 eV. The changes of N binding energy of three different materials may be caused by the connection of functional groups on the surface [41].

SEM and TEM were conducted to observed the morphology and internal structure of the materials. As displayed in Fig. 3 (a-c), the CZIS@CQDS-T presented a compact flap-shaped composite flower bulb structure, with the diameter mainly distributed in the range of 2–6 μm. Dense petal shape was conducive to provide more loading sites for CQDs and more reactive sites, which could effectively promote photocatalytic activity. The internal structure of the material was observed by TEM (Fig. 3 (d-g)), and the flower spherical structure assembled by many nanosheets was consistent with SEM. As shown in Fig. 3 (g), a large number of CQDs with a diameter of about 2–5 nm were uniformly distributed at the edge of CZIS@CQDS-T. Edge-high-density quantum dots exposed more active sites for reactions, thus promoting reactants adsorption and activation. As marked in the HRTEM (Fig. 3 (h)), the lattice spacing of 0.30 nm was corresponded to (102) crystal surface of the CZIS [31]. Besides, lattice spacing 0.21 nm was corresponded to graphitic carbon (100) crystal surface, indicating that the carbon structure was partially graphitized [41]. EDS results showed that all elements were evenly dispersed on the CZIS@CQDS-T (Fig. 3 (i)). The above results together demonstrated that the CZIS@CQDS-T was synthesized successfully.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and photoelectric performance of samples
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–vis DRS (Fig. 4 (a)) spectra showed a significant increase of light absorption capacity in visible light region after modification with CQDs and NCQDs, indicating that CQDs could improve light utilization capacity of photocatalysts. CQDs could convert low-energy photons into high-energy photons, effectively harnessing full spectrum lights. The light hunting efficiency (LHE) could reach more than 70% after loading CQDs, as compared with the CZIS (Fig. 4 (b)), suggesting the utilization rate of photons was greatly improved. These results illustrated that the loading of CQDs could improve photon capture and absorption, with the most significant enhancement in NCQDs using tryptophan as a nitrogen source. The Tauc chart (Fig. 4 (c)) showed the band gaps of all materials. According to the Kubelka-Munk function (αhv)n = K(hv-Eg) (Eqs (1)), the Eg of the CZIS, CZIS@CQDS and CZIS@CQDS-T were 2.35, 2.34 and 2.30 eV, respectively [42]. Mot-Schottky (M−S) test results (Fig. 4 (d)) suggested that CZIS, CZIS@CQDS and CZIS@CQDS-T were n-type semiconductors. The flat band potentials of the CZIS, CZIS@CQDS and CZIS@CQDS-T were −0.9 eV vs. Ag/AgCl (-0.7 eV vs. NHE), −0.89 eV vs. Ag/AgCl (-0.69 eV vs. NHE) and −0.9 eV vs. Ag/AgCl (-0.7 eV vs. NHE), respectively. Generally, the CB bottom of n-type the semiconductor was 0.1 ∼ 0.3 eV lower than the flat band [43,44]; hence, the CB potentials of the CZIS, CZIS@CQDS and CZIS@CQDS-T were calculated to be −0.8, −0.79, and −0.8 eV, respectively. Accordingly, the valence band tops of the CZIS, CZIS@CQDS and CZIS@CQDS-T were 1.55, 1.55 and 1.5 eV, respectively. The band-gap structures of the photocatalysts were shown in Fig. 4 (e). The above results indicated that NCQDs could effectively enhance light utilization without significantly altering band structure of the photocatalyst.

PL, I-T, EIS, and TRPL tests were conducted to characterized photoelectric properties of materials. PL (Fig. 5 (a)) showed that the fluorescence intensity decreased after loading CQDs, and the response intensity was even lower after loading NCQDs, indicating that the NCQDs could enhance carriers’ separation. As shown in Fig. 5 (b), the CZIS@CQDS-T exhibited the strongest photocurrent response (0.68 μA/cm2), which was 2.43 times of that of the CZIS (0.28μA/cm2). As shown in Fig. 5 (c), the CZIS@CQDS-T had the smallest arc radius and the lowest charge transfer resistance among all materials, which was most conducive to charges transfer [45]. TRPL was performed to evaluate the lifetime of photogenerated electrons, and results were shown in Fig. 5 (d)and Table S1. The CZIS@CQDS-T showed the longest charge lifetime (3.149 ns), indicating that CQDs with tryptophan as nitrogen source could improve carriers’ separation and transfer effectively.

To understand light absorption and photon conversion efficiency more clearly, incident photon-to-current conversion efficiency (IPCE) and absorbed photon-to-current conversion efficiency (APCE) were determined. Similar to the above photoelectrochemical results, all the CQDs modified materials exhibited higher IPCE and APCE than that of the CZIS, and the CQDs-T modified material showed the highest photon conversion efficiency in the region of 370–410 nm (Fig. 5 (e, f)). The results manifested that NCQDs could effectively accelerate the separation and transport of photogenerated carriers, thus ensuring more photo-induced electrons to participate in photoreduction reaction.

Fig. 6 (a) and (b) showed that the CZIS@CQDS-T exhibited the fastest photocatalytic CO2 reduction rate (with CO production rate of 70.69 μmol·g−1·h−1) within 60 min, followed by the CZIS@CQDS-U (CO production rate of 43.36 μmol·g−1·h−1), CZIS@CQDS-E (39.52 μmol·g−1·h−1), CZIS@CQDS (33.72 μmol·g−1·h−1) and CZIS (14.89 μmol·g−1·h−1). The CO production rate from the CZIS@CQDS-T was 4.77 times of that of CZIS. Negligible CH4 and H2 were detected from the system, and CO selectivity reached almost 100%. Besides, compared with other reported related modified semiconductors, the CZIS@CQDS-T showed better CO yield and higher selectivity than most reported materials (Table S2). The excellent CO2 reduction activity and selectivity of the CZIS@CQDS-T could be due to the excellent light absorption performance, efficient carrier separation and fast electron transfer caused by loading of NCQDs. Besides, the high content of pyridine nitrogen could promote electron accumulation on carbon rings as the reactive sites to enable more electrons to participate in CO2 reduction reaction.

The Fig. 6 (c) showed CO yields under different conditions. No product was produced under the condition of no light or no catalyst, indicating that CO was produced from CO2 photoreduction process on the illuminated photocatalysts. In the absence of TEOA and [Ru(bpy)3]Cl2·6H2O, there was no product either. Possible reason could be that TEOA and [Ru(bpy)3]Cl2·6H2O, acting as hole trapping agent and electron trapping medium respectively, making the reduction reaction continue to occur more easily[46]. To verify whether all the C element in CO came from CO2 rather than NCQDs, N2 and O2 were used to replace CO2 respectively. The results showed that no carbon-containing products were detected, indicating that all the CO came from CO2. When the reaction was performed without water, the reduction reaction was inhibited, manifesting that, as a source of protons, water played a crucial role in CO2 reduction process.

The stability of CZIS@CQDS-T was evaluated. The activity decreased slightly after 4 cycles of experiments, showing good stability (Fig. 6 (d)). In addition, as shown in Fig. S2 (a-d), the XPS, XRD, SEM and TEM of CZIS@CQDS-T after cycling were almost unchanged as compared with the fresh material, which further proved the great stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of photocatalytic reduction of CO2
ce:section.ce:para: To further explore the distinction in photoreduction performance of different catalysts, temperature programmed chemisorption (CO2-TPD and CO-TPD) was performed to test the amount of CO2 and CO desorption on various photocatalysts with increasing temperature. The result of CO2-TPD (Fig. 7 (a)) showed that the first desorption peak appeared at 51°, and the signal value of CZIS@CQDs-T was the weakest among all materials, indicating that the adsorption of CO2 was more stable. The second desorption peak began to appear when the temperature raised to 410°. It was obviously observed that CZIS@CQDs-T exhibited the largest peak area, which was almost twice of the CZIS. Due to the saturation adsorption before the desorption process, the larger detection signal of CO2 desorption at high temperature, the higher CO2 content adsorbed on the surface of the material. Therefore, it was proved that more efficient CO2 adsorption on the surface of CZIS@CQDs-T than those of CZIS and CZIS@CQDs. For CO-TPD (Fig. 7 (b)), the signal strength was contrary to that of CO2-TPD. The first desorption peak of CZIS appeared at 72°, and the signal peak of the CZIS@CQDs-T and CZIS@CQDs were delay to 101°. The signal intensity of CO desorption peak of the CZIS@CQDs-T and CZIS@CQDs was larger than that of the CZIS, indicating that CO was more easily desorbed. Surprisingly, at 421°, the CZIS showed an obvious desorption peak of CO, while the CZIS@CQDs and CZIS@CQDs-T showed almost obvious desorption signals, indicating that the CZIS@CQDs and CZIS@CQDs-T showed weak adsorption capacity of CO. The above results demonstrated that the CZIS loaded with NCQDs rich with pyridine nitrogen could significantly increase the adsorption capacity and adsorption capacity of CO2, and the desorption capacity of CO was stronger. That was the reason for the better CO2 reduction performance of the CZIS@CQDs-T.

In situ FT-IR (ISFTIR) was performed to further study the mechanism of CO2 reduction. The ISFTIR signals at different times of CZIS@CQDS-T were tested under dark adsorption and light conditions in CO2 atmosphere. As shown in Fig. 7 (c), compared with the blank background, a large amount of new signal peaks appeared in the dark adsorption process, including bicarbonate (HCO3 –, 1460, 1474 and 1543 cm−1), bidentate carbonate (b-CO3 2-, 1316 and 1653 cm−1), monodentate carbonate (m-CO3 2-, 1561 cm−1), and CO2*(1353, 1577, 1687, 1699 and 1718 cm−1) [8,47,48], indicating that CO2 was successfully adsorbed to catalyst surface and were activated at the adsorption sites by receiving electrons. As the adsorption time increased, the peak signal also increased gradually, indicating that CO2 adsorption was continuously adsorbed and activated on catalyst surface. The ISFTIR tests for photoreduction of CO2 showed different results compared to dark adsorption. As shown in Fig. 7 (d), in contrast to the main active substances (HCO3 –, b-CO3 2-, m-CO3 2- and CO2*) in the dark adsorption process, the signals intensity of b-CO3 2- (1318 cm−1), m-CO3 2- (1507 and 1520 cm−1), HCO3 – (1419 cm−1) and CO2* (1365 cm−1) were significantly weakened in photoreduction process, indicating the successful transformation into intermediates [49–51]. The intermediate of COOH* (1541 and 1559 cm−1) showed the strongest signal, indicating that the activated groups on the surface were effectively converted into product intermediates during the photoreduction process [31]. It was worth noting that COOH* was a key intermediate for CO production, and its existence ensured the efficacious conversion of CO2 to CO. In addition, the appearance of CO* proved the successful generation of CO. According to CO-TPD analysis, the reason for the weak signal value of CO* may be that CO* was hardly to adsorb on catalyst surface for further reaction, thus generating CO. The weak signal of CHO* also confirmed this conclusion. Generally, adsorbed CO* would further bind electrons to produce CHO* and CH3O*, which were the key intermediates for CO* to further produce CH4. No signal peak corresponding to CH3O* could be observed in the ISFTIR, indicating that the reaction process from CO* to CH4 was limited. The reason was rapid desorption from photocatalyst surface during the conversion of COOH* to CO. This phenomenon was consistent with the result of CO-TPD. Interestingly, water peaks were also observed at 1652 cm−1, indicating the formation of H2O during the photoreaction of CO2.

Based on the above results, possible photoreduction pathways of CO2 to CO were deduced. Firstly, in the dark adsorption process, CO2 combined with H2O to form CO3 2–, due to the saturated CO2 gas chamber, CO2 concentration was higher to form a high concentration of carbonic acid; while H2CO3 was a weak acid, in this case only one H+ could be ionized, which in turn facilitated generation of HCO3 –. Part of the surface adsorbed CO2 was bound to pyridine nitrogen, due to the presence of lone pairs of electrons in pyridine nitrogen, CO2 would bind electrons to be activated to become CO2 –. During light exposure, CO2 –, b-CO3 2– and m-CO3 2– were converted to COOH* under the combination of H+ and e-. Then CO was generated through the COOH* intermediate. In addition, HCO3 – could be combined with OH– to form CO3 2–, which could further react with e- and H+ to form H2O and CO. All reaction steps are as follows (Eqs (2–10)).
CZIS@CQDS-T + hv → h+ + e- (2)
CO2 + H2O → H2CO3 (3)
CO3 2– + CO2 + H2O → 2HCO3 – (4)
HCO3 – + OH–→CO3 2– + H2O (5)
CO2 + e- →CO2* (6)
CO2*+ e- + H+→HCOO* (7)
COOH* + e- + H+→CO∗ + H2O (8)
CO∗→CO ↑ (9)
CO3 2–+ 2e- + 2H+→2OH– + CO ↑ (10)

To further investigate the photoreduction mechanism, in-situ XPS was carried out to probe deeply into the possible reaction sites. Fig. 8 showed the orbitals mapping of Zn, In, S, Cu and C in the CZIS@CQDS and CZIS@CQDS-T under light and dark conditions. The binding energy increased implied that the surface loses electrons, whereas if the surface gained electrons, the binding energy became smaller [52]. Comparing the shift of different element binding energy in the CZIS@CQDS-T after illumination, the binding energies of Zn, In, S and Cu became larger, which shifted in the range of 0.15 to 0.35 eV. Comparing C orbitals, the binding energy of C 1S orbital tended to become smaller when irradiated. The result indicated that C-C, C = N and C-N bond obtained electrons, and the binding energy changed even more in carbon which achieved more electrons. This phenomenon illustrated that the path of electron transfer in the CZIS@CQDS-T was from the CZIS to NCQDs. In the CZIS@CQDs, Zn was the atom where the electron transfer occurred mainly in Zn3In2S6, and there was no significant binding energy shift between In and S elements, indicating that no obvious electron transfer occurred. In contrast to the CZIS@CQDS-T, the binding energy of Cu 2p became larger which demonstrated loss of electrons on Cu atom. The binding energy of C-C bond in C 1S orbital became smaller, consistent with CZIS@CQDS-T, which meant electrons were accumulating on the surface. The above results illustrated that the loading of CQDs could lead to directional transfer of photogenerated electrons and the accumulation of electrons from the CZIS to CQDs. In addition, the larger shift in binding energy indicated the NCQDs loading could better separate photogenerated carriers and induced more electrons transfer to the NCQDs. The higher the electron density of CQDs as the reaction active site, the higher the CO2 activation and conversion efficiency, which facilitated the photoreduction reaction to proceed.

The differential charge density and the adsorption energy could provide further insights into the mechanism. Since NCQDs contained pyridine nitrogen, pyrrole nitrogen and graphite nitrogen, modeling analysis cannot be carried out. To determine which structure was beneficial to photocatalytic reduction, three structures were attached respectively on the CZIS to conduct simulation calculation analysis. The CO2 adsorption energy of three nitrogen structure were shown in Fig. 9 (a-c). Pyridine nitrogen had the highest adsorption energy for CO2 (-0.724 eV), indicating that CO2 adsorbed stably on the surface of the structure. The differential charge density calculated the change in electron density during the adsorption of CO2. The blue and yellow area represented charge accumulation and charge depletion, respectively. As shown in Fig. 9 (d-f), pyridine nitrogen structure exhibited denser charge density in the region of CO2 adsorption, indicating that pyridine nitrogen could accumulate electrons more efficiently and enhance CO2 activation. The Bader value could reflect the surface electron aggregation ability, △q was the Bader value when adsorbed CO2, and the more negative value, the more electrons were gained by CO2. The △q of graphitic nitrogen, pyrrole nitrogen and pyridine nitrogen structures were −0.08, −0.06 and −0.07 eV, respectively, indicating that pyridine nitrogen accumulated more electrons during the adsorption of CO2. Through the above results, charge accumulation mainly occurred on NCQDs, it could effectively promote electron transfer on the CZIS@CQDs-T composite. The excellent ability of charge transfer and accumulation could efficiently promote photoreduction of CO2.

The adsorption energy of CO2, CO and CHO* on pyridine nitrogen limbed structure was calculated to verify the reason of high selective reduction. As shown in Fig. 10 , the adsorption energies of CO2, CO and CHO* were −0.724, −0.143 and −0.087 eV, respectively. The N lone pair electrons of pyridine nitrogen could accept protons and become basic. Under the action of Lewis, the adsorption of CO2 was significantly improved. And weak CO and CHO* adsorption energy led to easy desorption to limit CH4 formation. The stable CO2 adsorption and easy desorption of intermediates effectively improve the selectivity of products.

Based on the above analysis, the mechanism of CO2 reduction was deduced. The CO2-TPD and calculation of adsorption energy proved that a large amount of CO2 could be more efficiently adsorbed on the pyridine nitrogen structure, which was beneficial to further CO2 activation. The difference charge results showed that the loading of CQDs could effectively improve electrons accumulation during CO2 adsorption, and a electron-rich environment could facilitate the activation of the absorbed CO2 into CO2* and accelerate intermediates reconstruction. In the intermediate’s reconstruction process, CO2* would combine with hydrolyzed protons and photoelectrons to form COOH*, and COOH* combined with electrons and H+ to further generate CO* and H2O. In addition, HCO3 – combined with OH– to produce CO3 2–, which reacted with electrons and H+ to form CO*. According to the CO-TPD and CO adsorption energy, low CO adsorption capacity and weak adsorption energy made CO rapidly desorb on material surface, retarding the formation of CHO* and CH3O*, thus limiting the formation of CH4. The phenomenon was consistent with the weak signal of CHO* in DRFTIR. Another explanation for the high selectivity of CO generation rather than CH4 could be that the formation of CO requires only 2 electrons while CH4 requires 8 electrons (CO2 + 8H+ + 8e → CH4 + 2H2O Eqs (14)), which was more dynamically favorable compared to CH4 generation. Considering the above factors, the regulation of pyridine nitrogen in CQDs content can effectively improve the electron accumulation on NCQDs, enhance adsorption and activation of CO2 on the composite catalyst, hence, the loading of NCQDs could effectively improve CO2 photoreduction rate and selectivity. The mechanism of photocatalytic reduction CO2 over the CZIS@CQDs-T was illustrated in Scheme 1 .

In this study, the Cu0.05Zn2.95In2S6 was modified with NCQDs which containing different contents of pyridine nitrogen, pyrrole nitrogen and graphite nitrogen, and the performances of photocatalytic CO2 reduction of the modified composites were determined. Results showed that the CZIS@CQDs-T with higher content of pyridine nitrogen exhibited a CO yield of up to 70.691 μmol·g−1·h−1, with product selectivity of 100%. The CO yield of the CZIS@CQDs-T was 4.77 times of the Cu0.05Zn2.95In2S6. The excellent CO2 reduction performance was attributed to the stronger light capture ability, more efficient charges separation, faster electron directional transfer and excellent CO2 adsorption and activation with the assistance of NCQDs containing more pyridine nitrogen sites. Based on results of ISFTIR, TPD, in situ XPS and DFT calculation, the CO2 reduction pathway and mechanism were proposed. This study provides reference for designing biomass-derived CQDs modified photocatalysts for realization of efficient photoreduction of CO2.
